item.title: FirstFT: Japanese bank SMFG explores buying US investment bank Jefferies
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Anthropic vs the Pentagon, and Apollo limits redemptions from flagship private credit fund

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Israel Reports Missile Damage in Tel Aviv
item.description: President Trump said there had been “very strong talks” with Iran to end the war, though Iranian officials did not confirm that. Israel continued its campaign against Hezbollah in Lebanon.

item.title: UK voting system needs updating for age of personalised politics
item.description: John Major’s mocked 1990s ‘cones hotline’ demonstrates why voters today increasingly gravitate to smaller parties

item.title: Ukraine Spent Big to Shield Energy Industry From Drones. Is the Mideast Next?
item.description: With the use of electronic jamming systems and interceptor drones, the Ukrainian national oil and gas company may be a model for others.

item.title: North Korea’s Kim hits out at US ‘aggression’ amid Iran war
item.description: Pyongyang issues implicit rebuke over Middle East conflict as leader vows to uphold ‘irreversible’ nuclear status

item.title: Government Cuts Gut the Memory of Argentina’s Dirty War
item.description: Fifty years after the military dictatorship, Argentina’s government is defunding human rights groups and promoting a revisionist account of the junta’s crimes.

item.title: Australia and EU agree sweeping trade deal in face of global uncertainty
item.description: Australia and the EU sign sweeping trade and security deals after years of negotiations.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
Trump Sours on British Leader Over Iran War: ‘What If Donald Shouts at Me?’
item.description: President Trump once called Prime Minister Keir Starmer a friend. But Mr. Starmer’s decision not to join the attacks on Iran has led to merciless mocking by the president.

item.title: Japan’s SMFG explores possible takeover of Jefferies
item.description: Sumitomo Mitsui’s banking unit holds minority stake in US investment bank

item.title: Europe Clinches Critical Mineral Access With Australian Trade Deal
item.description: The European Union and Australia have shaken hands on a trade deal that would improve access to aluminum, lithium and other critical minerals for the 27-nation bloc.

item.title: Sinner surpasses Djokovic's Masters 1000 record
item.description: Jannik Sinner surpasses Novak Djokovic's 10-year record for consecutive sets won at ATP Masters 1000 events in his Miami Open win over Corentin Moutet.

item.title: Tango Therapy: How the Dance of Passion Is Helping Parkinson’s Patients
item.description: Once a week, patients in an Argentine hospital with Parkinson’s disease use the movements of tango to help address issues of balance, stiffness and coordination.

item.title: Do markets believe the president?
item.description: Plus, gold as unsafe haven

item.title: Apollo agrees biggest Japan deal in $3.7bn rescue of glassmaker NSG
item.description: Japanese manufacturer has struggled since swooping for UK rival Pilkington two decades ago

item.title: Danish premier Frederiksen poised to retain power as right splinters
item.description: Also in this newsletter: How to tackle Europe’s air defence shortage

item.title: Cuba's mothers-to-be prepare to give birth in a country plunged into darkness
item.description: Two pregnant women tell the BBC's Will Grant of their hopes and fears as their nation is mired in crisis.

item.title: China dials back on fuel price hikes to 'reduce burden' on drivers
item.description: It comes as countries across the region are taking various measures to weather the soaring cost of fuel.

item.title: Federal Reserve will stay on hold if energy shock is protracted
item.description: Read the March forecast from the FT’s Monetary Policy Radar team

item.title: Why the BTS Comeback Concert Was a ‘Disaster’ for Some Businesses
item.description: The turnout for the K-pop titans’ show was much lower than projected by officials, hitting the bottom line of some restaurants. Shares in the group’s management company also fell.

item.title: Is Hollywood’s $111bn megadeal Mission Impossible?
item.description: Now comes the hard part for David Ellison’s audacious deal to combine Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery

item.title: Hormuz fertiliser block will upend world’s food production
item.description: Any ceasefire negotiations must include a humanitarian carve-out for such shipments

item.title: Civic-minded pensioners make the world go round
item.description: Policymakers need to think about the costs of having a generation of retirees not on the property ladder

item.title: The secret to Ukraine’s remarkably resilient labour market
item.description: Flexibility, financial support and remote work have offset some of the economic upheaval of war

item.title: Banks and claims specialists set to challenge £11bn car finance redress
item.description: FCA to unveil scheme to compensate millions of customers of allegedly mis-sold car loans next week

item.title: Zelenskyy row with MPs jeopardises reforms linked to IMF and EU loans
item.description: Ukrainian parliament at odds with president over tax and anti-corruption measures

item.title: Palantir turns poisonous on the campaign trail
item.description: Donald Trump’s unpopular immigration crackdown has made links to the Peter Thiel-backed company a liability for candidates

item.title: The economic cost of Trump’s migrant crackdown
item.description: The US president’s immigration policies could hit construction, agriculture and hospitality as well as consumer spending

item.title: War sparks ‘panic buying’ of aluminium among global carmakers
item.description: Disruption to power supplies and shipping bottlenecks have affected key sources of production in Gulf

item.title: Siemens boss says Europe risks ‘disaster’ from prioritising AI independence
item.description: Roland Busch warns against throttling ‘innovation speed for the sake of creating sovereignty’

item.title: Indonesia targets investment banks for ‘alleged capital market crimes’
item.description: Regulators probe underwriters UOB, Mirae and Shinhan after January’s plunge in stock prices

item.title: Netanyahu’s rivals try to outdo him over Iran
item.description: Israel’s opposition parties criticise prime minister’s management of war even as they express strong support for offensive

item.title: Hasan Piker Defends Upscale Lodging During Humanitarian Mission in Cuba
item.description: He helped bring attention to a group delivering humanitarian aid to Cuba, which was criticized for staying in an upscale hotel as Cubans faced another blackout.

item.title: Pakistan Dials Up Its Information War
item.description: New, friendly media operations and expanded state-run television are pushing Pakistan’s message while independent news outlets face repression.

item.title: A Choice of Deadly Drones Is Only a Few Clicks Away for Ukrainian Troops
item.description: Ukraine has created online marketplaces to let units select their own drones, a break from generations of standardized and centralized weapons procurement.

item.title: How North Korea’s Kim Jong-un Is Using the Iran War to Justify His Nuclear Arsenal
item.description: He delivered a lengthy speech at his country’s rubber-stamp Parliament, declaring that his nuclear power will shield his country from American hostility.

item.title: Colombian military plane crash kills at least 66
item.description: Colombia's defence minister says it was a "tragic accident" but the cause is not yet clear.

item.title: Chris Mason: Reeves to set out principles on who might get energy bills support - and who won't
item.description: The chancellor must react in real time to the economic shock of the war in Iran, writes the BBC's political editor.

item.title: UK must back North Sea oil and gas drilling, says trade body
item.description: The group says the country "urgently" needs to produce its own oil and gas to secure supplies.

item.title: Inside the alleged Russian operation to trigger anti-government protests in Angola
item.description: A group of Russians and Angolans are going on trial in Angola after being accused of fomenting protests, which they deny.

item.title: Trump Delays Threat to Iran, but War Negotiations Are in Early Stage
item.description: President Trump postponed his threat to strike power plants in Iran, citing “productive conversations” with the Iranians. But officials said the talks were in an early stage and not substantive.

item.title: UK universities flock to India - but will they succeed?
item.description: Nine UK universities are setting up campuses in India, but enrolment is expected to be modest initially.

item.title: Irish government to cut excise duty on diesel and petrol
item.description: Duty on diesel will be reduced by 20 cent and petrol will be cut by 15 cent from midnight on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Is Negotiating With Iran, Which Publicly Denies His Claim
item.description: “Very, very strong talks,” President Trump claimed. Fake news, replied an Iranian parliamentary leader.

item.title: Real talk is nonsense but I may coach again - Klopp
item.description: Former Liverpool manager Jurgen Klopp says speculation he will return to management with Real Madrid this summer is "nonsense".

item.title: Here’s What Happened in the War in the Middle East on Monday
item.description: Conflicting signals about whether any negotiations to end the fighting were in progress created confusion.

item.title: Burnham should be allowed to stand as an MP, says Nandy
item.description: The culture secretary questions her party's decision to block the Greater Manchester mayor from standing in a by-election.

item.title: Nigel Farage abandons walkabout due to protest
item.description: The Reform leader visits Felixstowe to launch his party's Suffolk local elections campaign.

item.title: Hong Kong government employee denies ordering surveillance of UK dissidents
item.description: Chung Biu "Bill" Yuen says he did not organise a "shadow policing" operation in London.

item.title: Giorgia Meloni’s big electoral setback in Italy
item.description: Her failed referendum gamble hints at growing dissatisfaction with her government

item.title: Referendum defeat leaves Italy's Meloni looking more vulnerable
item.description: Meloni vowed to press on after losing a vote on constitutional reform many turned into a referendum on her government.

item.title: Oil falls and shares rebound after Trump says talks have been held to end war
item.description: Energy prices fall and stock markets rebound after the US president says "very good and productive" talks have been held.

item.title: Canada’s Military Wants to Prove It Can Defend the High Arctic
item.description: Canadian soldiers transported M777 howitzers to the High Arctic to show their ability to fight in an increasingly contested part of the world. It did not go as planned.

item.title: PM warns against 'false comfort' of thinking Iran war will end quickly
item.description: The PM also tells senior MPs the conflict was "not our war" and there must be a "lawful basis" for any UK involvement.

item.title: Donald Trump’s latest climbdown suggests he wants to end the war
item.description: The divide between Israel and America is widening

item.title: Taiwan Debates Military Spending as Choices Over U.S. and China Loom
item.description: Tensions in the opposition Nationalist Party could surface this week as lawmakers argue over additional defense funding intended to counter Beijing’s growing might.

item.title: Caught Between Two Conflicts, Afghans Flee Iran
item.description: The war in Iran is choking off Afghanistan’s main economic lifeline and has forced at least 70,000 Afghan workers and students there back home to a nation embroiled in another conflict.

item.title: Italians Reject Judicial Overhaul, Undermining Meloni
item.description: Some 54 percent of voters spurned a government plan to change how jurists are supervised. It presented Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni with her biggest domestic challenge since her election in 2022.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Trump is scrambling for options in Iran
item.description: “To end the war on current terms would look like defeat [for Trump] to most of the world,” writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: A committed pharmacist and a homesick blogger – the Iranian civilians killed in the war
item.description: The toll on civilians is mounting fast as the US-Israeli war on Iran continues.

item.title: Ex-justice secretary to lead review into MP murder
item.description: Sir David Amess's daughter says the family will engage with the independent review in "good faith".

item.title: Slovenia becomes first EU country to introduce fuel rationing
item.description: Until further notice, motorists in Slovenia will be restricted to a maximum purchase of 50 litres of fuel per day.

item.title: Why the number of Islamic schools in Canada is soaring
item.description: Some Muslim parents worry about their children losing touch with their faith

item.title: Campaigners celebrate after new town plans dropped
item.description: Critics of plans to build a new town in Cheshire called it the "wrong proposal in the wrong place".

item.title: Ex-teacher accused of Scotland school abuse found guilty of sex attack in South Africa
item.description: A court in Cape Town found Iain Wares guilty of indecently assaulting a former student who accused him of sexual abuse in the 1980s.

item.title: Kenyan ex-foreign minister arrested and accused of staging his disappearance
item.description: The reported disappearance of Raphael Tuju had led to claims he may have been abducted.

item.title: Could Iranian missiles reach London and Paris?
item.description: Some experts say Iran may be able to launch missiles to hit London, but they would likely be shot down long before arriving and the risk is low.

item.title: HS2 trains could run slower than planned to save money
item.description: The government is looking at ways to make much-delayed London to Birmingham railway line cheaper to deliver.

item.title: Nigerian heritage key to my success - Itauma
item.description: Moses Itauma, one of heavyweight boxing's brightest prospects, says he found the answers to questions about his identity on a trip to his father's village in Nigeria.

item.title: US' and Iran's options for ending war narrow the longer it goes on
item.description: Iran shows no sign of climbdown, while air power alone cannot achieve the US' main goal.

item.title: Blast that injured two was 'terrorist attack', Ukrainian authorities say
item.description: The suspect is a 21-year-old local resident who claims he was blackmailed and forced to plant explosives.

item.title: Nigerian police arrest 15 after viral videos of alleged sexual assault at festival
item.description: The videos sparked widespread anger, leading to the hashtag #StopRapingWomen to trend on social media.

item.title: Ministers name seven areas where 'new towns' could be built
item.description: Six sites have been deprioritised from the flagship scheme to build a wave of new settlements.

item.title: France's former Socialist Prime Minister Lionel Jospin dies at 88
item.description: Jospin was head of government from 1997 to 2002 and is credited with bringing in the 35-hour working week.

item.title: Sidelined by War With Iran, Gaza Residents Remain in Limbo
item.description: The new war has led to panic buying and a surge in food prices for Gazans as they try to recover from Israel’s two-year offensive against Hamas.

item.title: Australia's ABC staff to go on strike for first time in 20 years
item.description: The 24-hour long strike is expected to disrupt live television and radio broadcasts.

item.title: HK police can now demand phone passwords under new national security rules
item.description: Those who do not comply can be jailed for up to a year and fined up to HK$100,000 ($12,700; £9,600).

item.title: Plan to scrap most short jail terms comes into effect
item.description: The government hopes it will ease the population crisis in prisons.

item.title: Germany has a shortage of workers - so it's turning to India for help
item.description: The European nation, struggling to find skilled staff, is giving jobs to young people from India.

item.title: France's Socialists hold on to power in major cities in election boost for mainstream
item.description: Paris and Marseille are held by the Socialists in local elections which saw the nationalist right win in Nice and certain towns.

item.title: Israeli settlers target Palestinian villages in occupied West Bank, attacking people and properties
item.description: The violence began after 18-year-old settler Yehuda Sherman was killed after reportedly being hit by a vehicle driven by a Palestinian while on his quad bike.

item.title: National blackout hits Cuba for second time in a week
item.description: Another total failure of its power grid underlines Cuba's hardship under a US-imposed fuel blockade.

item.title: Why did US and Israel attack Iran and how long could the war last?
item.description: Conflict has spread across the Middle East since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February.

item.title: Israel to expand ground and air attacks against Hezbollah in Lebanon
item.description: There are fears in Lebanon that Israel is preparing for a large-scale ground invasion.

item.title: Ukraine’s top drone commander wants to bleed Russia’s army dry
item.description: “Madyar” Brovdi has pioneered unmanned warfare methods NATO is yet to learn

item.title: Donald Trump has four bad options for the war in Iran
item.description: None is guaranteed to end the war or ease the economic crisis it has produced

item.title: Kenyans fighting illegally for Russia in Ukraine to be granted amnesty
item.description: It is illegal for Kenyans to serve in foreign armies and can carry a sentence of up to 10 years in jail.

item.title: Tributes paid to former Sunday Game presenter Michael Lyster
item.description: Michael Lyster was best known for presenting The Sunday Game Live for more than 30 years.

item.title: No assessment Iran could strike London, UK minister says
item.description: Steve Reed says there is "no specific assessment" Iran is capable of hitting the UK, after Israel claimed it now could.

item.title: Iranian missiles injure 180 in towns near Israeli nuclear site
item.description: Israel is investigating how ballistic missiles got through the country's sophisticated air defences.

item.title: Why Bangalore has India’s best billionaires
item.description: Its philanthropists want to do more than just give money away

item.title: Hillsborough campaigners blame Mahmood for law delay
item.description: The Hillsborough Law has been further delayed and will not be passed in this session of parliament.

item.title: Qatari and Turkish citizens die in military helicopter crash
item.description: Four Qatari military personnel, one Turkish serviceman and two technicians from a Turkish defence company were on board.

item.title: Sudan drone attack on key hospital killed 64 people during Eid, WHO says
item.description: The strike killed 64 people, including 13 children, two nurses and a doctor, according to the WHO head.

item.title: 'It's been bittersweet': The Iranian-Iraqi cafe drawing crowds to Hackney
item.description: Logma, a newly opened Iranian-Iraqi cafe in Hackney, has gone viral for its sandwiches.

item.title: How a medieval English abbey became a Chinese wedding destination
item.description: Today's brides say they are inspired by wedding of Taiwanese popstar Jay Chou at the abbey in 2015.

item.title: BBC in Cuba as island plunged into darkness
item.description: More than 10 million people are enduring power cuts after Cuba's national electrical grid collapsed again.

item.title: Foreign secretary denounces 'reckless Iran threats' after missiles fired at Diego Garcia
item.description: Iran reportedly fired two ballistic missiles at Indian Ocean base, but neither reached the target.

item.title: 'Peace is a gradual thing': How land, cattle and identity fuel a deadly Nigerian conflict
item.description: How a lack of justice and trust in the security forces perpetuates deadly violence in Plateau state.

item.title: Trump at a crossroads as US weighs tough options in Iran
item.description: Statements from President Trump that the war is almost over are not matching up with the reality on the ground.

item.title: Pop megastars BTS electrify historic centre of Seoul with comeback concert
item.description: The world's biggest band returns after more than three years with a huge concert in the South Korean capital.

item.title: Did Australia's under-16s social media ban work?
item.description: Three months since it came into effect, teenagers tell the BBC how the world-first law has impacted their lives so far.

item.title: Rwanda batter becomes youngest women's T20 centurion
item.description: Rwanda's Fanny Utagushimaninde says it was like "a dream" after becoming the youngest woman to make a T20 international century at the age of 15 years and 223 days.

item.title: Why there's no sign of the conflict in the Middle East 'winding down'
item.description: Iran's foreign minister said that it doesn't appear that the US is prepared to halt its aggression after President Trump said the US was considering "winding down" its war.

item.title: BTS make live return in front of huge crowd
item.description: RM, Jin, Suga, J-Hope, Jimin, V and Jung Kook perform together for the first time since October 2022.

item.title: US lifts sanctions on some Iranian oil as energy prices soar
item.description: The treasury secretary says the move will quickly bring about 140m barrels of oil to global markets.

item.title: UK allows US to use bases to strike Strait of Hormuz targets
item.description: Downing Street says ministers approved the expansion after accusing Iran of "reckless strikes".
